resources.string: Next
Close
Image capture
Tips about optimal image capture

When capturing for an image condition, you need to avoid changing parts such as the background. The capture needs to look as close as possible to what will be displayed when detecting.

Try to always capture as close as possible to the target you want to match while keeping enough details for detection.

Note: You can pinch to zoom to the capture and control more precisely the area.

Be careful with images containing transparent areas. If the background behind the transparent surface changes, the detection algorithm will detect the changes in color or shape and will not consider it as detected. 

Try to avoid transparent parts in your condition while keeping enough static details for detection. You can also change the detection tolerance in order to compensate for the possible changes in small transparent areas. 

If there is a text within the target you want to match, you might want to consider using a Text Condition, as they are less impacted by background change noise.

Image detection is more accurate when applied to things to detect that have a lot of borders. It is also faster if the image is smaller, but it will lose confidence if the image is too small and lacks details. Try to keep that in mind when creating new image conditions.\n\nIf you are trying to detect very small things, you might want to use one or several Color Conditions instead, they are more reliable for such cases, and way faster.
Detection area
Learn about the impact on your scenario performances

When creating an Image Condition, you can define where the detection will occur on the screen by changing the detection type.

By default, a new Image Condition will always detect at the same spot as the capture. This is the fastest way for detecting an image. 

If the image you need to detect can be displayed at several places, you then need to use one of the other options.

When selecting \"In area\", the area selection field will enable; allowing you to define a detection area.\n\nBy default, the area will be the same as the capture area, but you can move and expand it. The bigger the area is, the slower the detection will be, so try to only include the interesting parts of the screen.
The last option, \"Whole screen\" will change the detection area to the whole display. \n\nThis is by far the slowest detection type and you should use it only as a last resort.
Text detection area
Tips about optimal text detection

When detecting a text on the screen, you need to define a detection area that will be scanned for text during detection. 

When detecting static text, you want the area to be close to the text while keeping a small margin around it. The text should not touch the borders of the area or the detection confidence will drop significantly. 

Note: for very small text, you might need to increase the detection quality of your scenario, as higher resolution will help the detection of those.

When detecting a text that can appear in multiple positions, use the smallest detection area possible. The bigger the detection area is, the slower the detection will be. \n\nIf the different positions are known and not random, it might be faster to use separate text conditions for each position than one with a very big area. You can test your timings using the debug report if you are into deep optimization of your scenario timings.
Number detection area
Tips about optimal number detection

The tips here are the same as for the Text Condition: when detecting a number on the screen, you need to define a detection area that will be scanned for numbers during detection. 

When detecting a static number, you want the area to be close to the number while keeping a small margin around it. The number should not touch the borders of the area or the detection confidence will drop significantly. 

Note: for very small numbers, you might need to increase the detection quality of your scenario, as higher resolution will help the detection of those.
Screen condition types
Learn which one to use depending on your use case

Color condition is the fastest way of detecting with Klick\'r. It simply verifies the color of a pixel on your screen. \n\nThis condition is convenient when trying to detect simple UI elements such as a button color or progress bar completion, or small changes.

Image condition is slower than a color condition, but it allows more flexibility for the screen content detection. Execution time is pretty low when detecting in a small area, but it can greatly increase when the area is bigger. \n\nThis condition is convenient when trying to detect clear shapes and colors, like the content of a card, or an icon.

Text conditions and Number conditions both use the same detection process. 

Text detection is the slowest detection type, but unlike the image detection, it is not sensitive to transparency and allows more precision in complex detection scenes. Execution time increase with detection area size and also depends on the alphabet selected. 

This condition is convenient when trying to detect a text or a number independently of how it is displayed.
Detection confidence
Tips about detection confidence

Klick\'r does a lot of pre-processing before looking for your screen condition in order to speed up the process and reduce battery impact. This creates uncertainties and approximations in the results that are expressed as the confidence score. \n\nTo ensure your conditions are working as expected, you can test them individually by pressing the play arrow button while focusing a condition on the screen condition list.

When testing a screen condition, the detection confidence score will be displayed, and you can modify the tolerated difference directly here, allowing you to get rid of false positive or be more flexible with allowed results.

Detection results are also impacted by the detection resolution. Available in the Scenario dialog, it allows you to change how much Klick\'r downscales your screen content before processing it. \n\nThis can impact the confidence score of all of your screen conditions, with higher resolution meaning better detection, but also slower execution time.
On Broadcast Received
Listen to broadcasts from other apps

On Android, when applications want to share their state with other apps, they use a Broadcast Intent. \n\nWith the \"On Broadcast Received\" condition, you can monitor those states and react with an action in Klick\'r. Some default values for the Android system state are provided, but keep in mind that broadcast conditions are more for developers, as mastering them requires Android development knowledge.
On Counter Reached
React to your scenario\'s counter values

Counters are variables declared in your scenario that can be modified, displayed or verified during its execution. 

With the "On Counter Reached" condition, you can verify the current value of a counter to trigger actions. All mathematical comparison operators are available to customize the verification. 

Counters can be used in various ways and using a single counter-related condition or action is often not enough to use them correctly. Interactive tutorials covering the Counter feature are available in their dedicated section.
